rule.desc: Calling an async function without `await` or an attached rejection handler, leaving the Promise to reject unobserved.
rule.better: . Always `await` promises or attach a `.catch()` (or equivalent) handler. Use the `void` operator only for intentional fire-and-forget calls where the Promise implementation handles errors internally or you rely on separate/global error handling.
rule.why_bad: Unhandled promise rejections can terminate or crash Node.js processes (depending on runtime version and configuration) and are typically only logged and then ignored in browsers.

rule.desc: Mixing callback-style and Promise-style APIs in the same flow.
rule.better: Wrap callback APIs with `util.promisify` or `new Promise()` at the boundary, then use async/await throughout.
rule.why_bad: Mixed styles produce confusing error handling, nested callbacks inside async functions, and easy-to-miss unhandled errors.

rule.desc: Passing `async` functions to event handlers or callbacks that expect synchronous `void` return.
rule.better: Wrap async logic in a non-async handler that catches and handles errors explicitly.
rule.why_bad: The caller ignores the returned Promise, so rejections go unhandled. DOM event handlers (for example, `addEventListener`) and some framework callbacks ignore returned Promises, depending on version.

rule.desc: Sharing mutable state between async operations without coordination.
rule.better: . Use immutable data, message passing, or explicit synchronization (mutex/semaphore).
rule.why_bad: Concurrent async operations can interleave, producing race conditions even in single-threaded JavaScript when they share mutable variables.

rule.desc: Wrapping an existing Promise in `new Promise()` unnecessarily.
rule.better: Return the existing Promise directly or use async/await.
rule.why_bad: The extra wrapper adds complexity and can swallow errors if `reject` is not called properly.

rule.desc: Sequentially awaiting independent Promises instead of running them concurrently.
rule.why_bad: Sequential awaits add up latency linearly. Independent fetches that take 200ms each take 400ms sequentially vs 200ms concurrently.
